behaviors.description: Speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees
behaviors.justification: Testing speaker label preservation requires calling the real speech-to-text API to verify it supports speaker diarization and returns labels in a format the service can process. Mocking the API response would hide failures if the live API doesn't support diarization, returns labels differently, or has inconsistent speaker tracking. Additionally, verifying that labels flow through to the LLM summary and enable correct attribution requires a real LLM call. The critical failure mode—"live API doesn't support speaker labels or the service doesn't handle real API output"—is only catchable at System level.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare a real audio file with 2-3 distinct speakers (or use a public meeting recording). Call the real speech-to-text API with speaker diarization enabled. Verify the transcript includes speaker labels (e.g., 'Speaker 1:', 'Speaker 2:') with clear segment boundaries. Verify the service extracts these labels without loss or modification. Pass the labeled transcript to the real LLM and verify the structured summary attributes statements to specific speakers by name or role (e.g., 'John (Product Lead) said...', 'Maria (Engineer) decided...').

behaviors.description: Structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting
behaviors.justification: Action item extraction and attribution is a quality judgment made by the LLM, not deterministic service logic. Whether the LLM correctly identifies all attendees assigned tasks and extracts accurate action items per attendee depends entirely on real LLM non-determinism and reasoning quality. Mocking the LLM to always return configured action items would hide real failures where the live LLM misses action items, conflates multiple attendees, or misattributes tasks. The failure—'LLM failed to extract an action item for an assigned attendee'—can only be caught with a real LLM call.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare a meeting transcript where 3–4 attendees are explicitly assigned distinct, unambiguous tasks (e.g., 'Alice will prepare the Q2 report', 'Bob will share the slides by Friday', 'Carol will schedule the follow-up'). Call the real LLM with a prompt to extract and structure action items. Verify the returned summary contains an 'action_items' field. Verify each task-assigned attendee has at least one action item entry. Verify action items are correctly attributed (attendee name matches, task matches stated assignment). Run this test multiple times with the same transcript to verify consistency (or document expected variance if the LLM is non-deterministic).

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative
behaviors.justification: Decision extraction and proper section placement depend on LLM judgment about what constitutes a 'decision' and how to categorize it in the output schema. Mocking the LLM response would hide real failures where the live LLM buries decisions in a narrative field, misclassifies decisions as general notes, or misses decisions entirely. The failure—'LLM placed a key decision in the wrong section or missed it'—is a quality judgment only catchable with a real LLM call. Different LLM models or versions may extract decisions differently, reinforcing that Agentic testing is required.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare a meeting transcript containing 3–4 explicit decisions (e.g., 'We decided to postpone the launch until Q3', 'We agreed on using Cloud Provider X', 'We chose Agile over Waterfall'). Call the real LLM with instructions to extract decisions into a 'decisions' field, separate from narrative or other fields. Verify the returned summary has a 'decisions' section. Verify each decision appears in the decisions section (not in narrative or other fields). Verify decisions are captured accurately without paraphrasing that changes meaning. Run the test multiple times to understand LLM consistency on this classification.

behaviors.description: When audio is longer than 60 minutes, service splits it into segments and processes sequentially, merging results into a single coherent summary
behaviors.justification: Testing long-audio handling requires real audio files (>60 minutes) to verify the service actually detects length and splits correctly. Real API calls are needed to see how speech-to-text and LLM APIs handle partial transcripts, segment boundaries, and context loss across splits. Mocking APIs would hide real failures: segment processing might fail due to API timeouts, format mismatches, or context gaps in partial audio. Additionally, verifying coherence of merged summaries (no duplicates, logical flow across segments, correct action item consolidation) requires real LLM output for each segment and real merge logic. The failure—'service fails to process long audio or produces incoherent merged summary'—spans both System infrastructure (segment handling, API calls) and Agentic quality (coherence).
behaviors.test_description: Prepare a real meeting recording or synthesized audio longer than 60 minutes (e.g., 75 minutes) with multiple speakers, clear decisions, and task assignments throughout. Call the service with this audio. Verify the service splits the audio into segments (expected size ~30 min or per service config). Verify each segment is transcribed separately via the real speech-to-text API. Verify each segment's transcript is independently summarized via the real LLM. Verify the summaries are merged into a single structured output. Verify the merged summary is coherent: no duplicate action items, no contradictory decisions, decisions consistently attributed across segments, attendee action items correctly consolidated. Verify no manual intervention was required.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording
behaviors.justification: End-to-end pipeline testing requires a real meeting recording, real speech-to-text API calls, and real LLM calls to expose failures that only manifest in the fully integrated system. Mocking any component (API, audio file, storage) would hide real failures: dropped frames from large audio uploads, API timeouts or rate limits, LLM API failures, output serialization errors, or missing delivery steps. The failure—'pipeline hangs, fails silently, or requires manual intervention'—is only visible when all components interact with real I/O, real external APIs, and real async orchestration. This is System-level because it involves subprocess/HTTP calls, real audio files, and infrastructure behavior (timeouts, retries, delivery guarantees).
behaviors.test_description: Obtain or record a real meeting (audio only, 30–90 minutes, 2+ attendees). Upload the audio file through the service's public-facing API endpoint. Monitor the service without manual intervention. Verify the service receives the upload and begins processing. Verify the service calls the real speech-to-text API and receives a transcript. Verify the service calls the real LLM API and receives a structured summary with required fields (action items, decisions, attendees). Verify the service delivers the final output through an API response or file export without requiring user input, API retries, or manual data movement. Log and verify all external API calls succeeded and no step failed silently. Verify the entire pipeline completes in reasonable time (e.g., < 2× audio duration) with no hanging processes.
